Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館}}. These rare burial goods were other_designation1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 other_designation Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館}}. These rare burial goods were other_designation1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 other_designation Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:New2]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ogawadai Kofun Group}}

The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|12=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|12=draft}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|12=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:New2]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ogawadai Kofun Group}}

Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|12=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|12=draft}}. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|12=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:New2]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ogawadai Kofun Group}} {{Kofun navbar}}

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町|12=draft}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川|12=draft}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜|12=draft}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|12=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|12=draft}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|12=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:New2]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ogawadai Kofun Group}} {{Kofun navbar}}

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町|12=draft}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川|12=draft}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜|12=draft}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|12=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|12=draft}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|12=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] {{citation needed|Ogawadai Kofun Group}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{Kofun navbar}}

Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町|12=draft}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川|12=draft}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜|12=draft}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|12=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|12=draft}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|12=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] {{citation needed|Ogawadai Kofun Group}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] {{Kofun navbar}}

It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=draft|12=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|12=draft|12=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Ogawadai Kofun Group}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]]

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町|12=draft|12=draft}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川|12=draft|12=draft}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜|12=draft|12=draft}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|12=draft|12=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|12=draft|12=draft}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft|12=draft}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|12=draft|12=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|12=draft|12=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Ogawadai Kofun Group}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]]

Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Ogawadai Kofun Group}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]]

revision.comment: 17 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:小川台古墳群 up to rev 89953380
revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称=小川台古墳群 |画像= |所在地=千葉県山武郡横芝光町小川台 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |形状=前方後円墳.円墳.方墳 |規模=全長28m,30m,42m |築造年代=5世紀 - 7世紀（8世紀初頭） |出土品=形象埴輪等 |史跡指定= |有形文化財=町指定有形文化財
（出土埴輪）[https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html#a07 「歴史・伝統」横芝光町ホームページ] |埋葬施設= |地図2 = {{Location map|Japan Chiba|label=小川台
古墳群|lat_deg=35|lat_min=41|lat_sec=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''小川台古墳群'''（おがわだいこふんぐん）は、[[千葉県]][[山武郡]][[横芝光町]]小川台にある[[古墳群]]である。 == 概要 == 小川台古墳群は、[[九十九里浜]]中央部の[[栗山川]]中流東岸の舌状台地上に展開し、[[前方後円墳]]5基、[[円墳]]12基、[[方墳]]1基によって構成される。 このうち、1号墳（墳丘径28[[メートル]]の円墳）、2号墳（墳丘径21メートルの円墳）、3号墳（墳丘全長24メートルの前方後円墳）、4号墳（墳丘1辺約14メートルの方墳）、5号墳（墳丘全長30メートルの前方後円墳）について、[[1974年]]（[[昭和]]49年）に[[発掘調査]]が行われた。なお、本古墳群中最大のものは、見掛けの墳丘長42メートルの6号墳であるが、まだ調査は行われていない。 == 出土品 == 調査した[[古墳]]のうち、多くの[[遺物]]が出土したのは1号墳と5号墳である。 1号墳の3基の埋葬施設から、[[剣|鉄剣]]4振、鉄鉾1点、鉄鏃4点、鉄製刀子1点、滑石製有孔円板10点、臼玉39点、[[鉄鏃]]3点、鉄斧1点、滑石製臼玉151点以上が出土した。 5号墳の後円部頂の[[木棺]]からは、[[直刀|大刀]]5振、刀子1点、鉄鏃1点、鐙に伴う兵庫鎖、瑪瑙製勾玉1点、ガラス製小玉42点が出土した他、墳丘および周溝内からは、多量の[[円筒埴輪]]とともに人物埴輪や、馬形埴輪、鹿形埴輪、にわとり形埴輪、水鳥形埴輪等の動物埴輪、それに家形埴輪などの[[形象埴輪]]が検出された。出土状態の検討によって、形象埴輪列は北側中段の前方部分から後円部の前方部分まで樹てられていたとみられ、おそらく墳丘の一定区画内に限定して配置されていたものと考えられている。 === 人物埴輪 === 5号墳出土の人物埴輪で形状が確認できたものは、双脚[[挂甲]]武人像、双脚男子像、座る女子、壺を頭に載せる女子等の15点で、このうち性別が判明しているのは14点で男子9女子5であり、現在のところ千葉県最古の人物埴輪とされている。 これらの人物埴輪はいずれも無骨な造形で、かならずしも洗練された技法とは言いがたいが、造形の細部とくに[[衝角付冑]]や挂甲の仕様、髷の表現などは実物にかなり忠実に造形されていた。また、双脚挂甲武人像と双脚男子像には著しい特徴があった。同町中台の[[芝山古墳群]]・姫塚古墳出土の男子人物埴輪などに代表される双脚像は、台座を設けてその上に両足が載るように造形されているのがほとんどである。これに対し本古墳出土の双脚挂甲武人像と双脚男子像は、台座を設けず直接2本の足を墳丘に接地させており、同様なものとしては[[福島県]][[西白河郡]][[泉崎村]]原山1号墳の「力士像」と考えられる人物埴輪（[[福島県立博物館]]所蔵）が挙げられる程度で、他に例が無く希少な資料である。埴輪資料は2018年（平成30年）4月1日に町の[[有形文化財]]に指定された[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 文化遺産データベース]。 == 造営時期 == 造営時期として、1号墳の[[副葬品]]から[[5世紀]]代の年代が、5号墳の埴輪により5世紀末から[[6世紀]]前半の年代がうかがえる。4号墳出土の[[須恵器]]からは[[7世紀]]末ないし[[8世紀]]初頭の年代がうかがえ、須恵器については追葬に伴うものの可能性もあるが、4号墳が古墳時代終末期の方墳であることは間違いなく、全18基の古墳のうちの5基の調査のみではあるが、いずれにしろ長期間にわたって造営された古墳群であるといえる。 ==脚注== {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == *　[[大塚初重]]・小林三郎・熊野正也編『日本古墳大辞典』 [[東京堂出版]]、1989年。ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * 『千葉県の地名（日本歴史地名大系 12）』[[平凡社]]、1996年。ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 *　[[原島礼二]]・[[金井塚良一]] 編 『東国と大和王権』 [[吉川弘文館]]、1994年。ISBN 4-642-02186-8、141頁～ 「小川台五号墳の人物埴輪」の項 == 関連項目 == * [[古墳時代]] * [[芝山古墳・はにわ博物館]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html 芝山古墳・はにわ博物館 展示紹介 小川台5号墳] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわたいこふんくん}} [[Category:千葉県の古墳]] [[Category:横芝光町の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:円墳]] [[Category:古墳群]]
