{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Ogawadai Kofun Group}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]] {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]]

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in {{ill|Yokoshibahikari|ja|横芝光町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoshibahikari|qq=}}, Sanbu District, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiba Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuriyama River|qq=}}, in the central part of the {{ill|Kujukuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujukuri Beach|qq=}} region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in {{ill|Izumizaki Village|ja|泉崎村|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumizaki Village|qq=}}, Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the {{ill|Fukushima Prefectural Museum|ja|福島県立博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushima Prefectural Museum|qq=}}. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded {{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|qq=}} suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|ja|芝山古墳・はにわ博物館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]]

ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]]

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:千葉県の古墳]]

The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese Kofuns in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:横芝光町の歴史]]

Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:横芝光町の歴史]]

The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. revision.comment: Merge [[Category:横芝光の Municipal History]] into [[Category:Municipal History of Yokoshibahikari]] via bot
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal History of Yokoshibahikari]]

The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=|1=Hatsushige Ōtsuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433969}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=|1=Reiji Harashima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409594}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Ogawadai Kofun Group]] [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=|1=Hatsushige Ōtsuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433969}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=|1=Reiji Harashima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409594}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q11460398}}

So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=|1=Hatsushige Ōtsuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433969}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=|1=Reiji Harashima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409594}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal History of Yokoshibahikari]] [[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11460398}}

This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|qq=|1=Hatsushige Ōtsuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433969}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|qq=|1=Reiji Harashima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409594}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal History of Yokoshibahikari]] {{wikidata link|Q11460398}}

Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Japanese Kofun | name = Ogawadai Kofun Group | image = | location = Ogawadai, Yokoshibahikari Town, Sanbu District, Chiba Prefecture | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E|region:JP_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} | type = Keyhole-shaped tumuli, round tumuli, square tumuli | area = 28 m, 30 m, 42 m | built = 5th century – 7th century (early 8th century) | burial goods = Haniwa, others | alternate_name = Town-Historic Site1 tangible cultural property (haniwa burial goods) [https://www.town.yokoshibahikari.chiba.jp/soshiki/14/1395.html=a07 "History and Traditions" Yokoshibahikari Town Homepage] | burial burials = | map = {{location map|Japan Chiba|map_dot_label=Ogawadai
Kofun Group|coordinates=35|coordinates=41|coordinates=20|lon_deg=140|lon_min=29|lon_sec=40|marksize=10|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|41|20|N|140|29|40|E}}}} }} '''Ogawadai Kofun Group''' (小川台古墳群) is a cluster of ancient Kofuns (kofun) located in [[:en:Yokoshibahikari|Yokoshibahikari]], Sanbu District, [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. == Overview == The Ogawadai Kofun Group is situated on a tongue-shaped plateau along the eastern midstream of the [[:en:Kuriyama River|Kuriyama River]], in the central part of the [[:en:Kujūkuri Beach|Kujukuri Beach]] region. It consists of 5 keyhole-shaped tumuli, 12 round tumuli, and 1 square tumulus. Among these, Kofun No. 1 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 28 meters), Kofun No. 2 (a round tumulus with a diameter of 21 meters), Kofun No. 3 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 24 meters), Kofun No. 4 (a square tumulus with a side length of approximately 14 meters), and Kofun No. 5 (a keyhole-shaped tumulus with a total length of 30 meters) were excavated in 1974. The largest mound in the group, Kofun No. 6, with a visible length of 42 meters, has yet to be investigated. == burial goods == Significant numbers of burial goods were uncovered during the excavations of Kofun No. 1 and Kofun No. 5. * '''Kofun No. 1:''' Three burial facilities yielded four iron swords, one iron spear, four iron arrowheads, one iron knife, ten perforated circular stone disks, 39 spindle-shaped beads, three iron arrowheads, one iron axe, and more than 151 spindle-shaped stone beads. * '''Kofun No. 5:''' burial goods discovered in a wooden coffin at the top of the rear circular portion include five long swords, one knife, one iron arrowhead, Hyōgo chains accompanying stirrups, one agate magatama (curved jewel), and 42 glass beads. The mound and surrounding moat contained numerous cylindrical haniwa, as well as human-shaped, horse-shaped, deer-shaped, chicken-shaped, and waterfowl-shaped animal haniwa, in addition to house-shaped figurines and other symbolic haniwa. Analysis of their placement suggests that symbolic haniwa were concentrated along the northern middle section, extending from the front part of the keyhole-shaped mound to the rear circular portion, and were likely confined to specific sections of the mound. === Human-shaped Haniwa Fifteen human haniwa were identified from Kofun No. 5, including figures of armored warriors standing with both legs, male figures standing with both legs, seated female figures, and women carrying jars on their heads. Of these, 14 haniwa were classified by gender, with 9 males and 5 females, making them the oldest known human haniwa in Chiba Prefecture. While these haniwa are somewhat rough in craftsmanship, their details—particularly the horned helmets, armor, and hair styles—demonstrate considerable fidelity to actual objects. Notably, the standing warrior and male figures are unique in that their feet are directly attached to the mound without a pedestal, a feature rarely seen elsewhere. Similar examples include the wrestler haniwa from Harayama Kofun No. 1 in [[:en:Izumizaki, Fukushima|Izumizaki Village]], Nishishirakawa District, Fukushima Prefecture, housed in the [[:en:Fukushima Museum|Fukushima Prefectural Museum]]. These rare burial goods were Historic Site1 as town tangible cultural properties on April 1, 2018.[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/419372 Historic Site Database] == built == The burial goods from Kofun No. 1 suggest a 5th-century construction date, while the haniwa from Kofun No. 5 indicate a period from the late 5th century to the early 6th century. Kofun No. 4 yielded [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] suggesting a late 7th-century to early 8th-century date, possibly associated with secondary burials. While only five of the 18 mounds have been excavated, the group reflects construction over an extended period, spanning from the 5th to the 8th century. == References == * {{ill|Hajime Otsuka|ja|大塚初重|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hajime Otsuka|1=Hatsushige Ōtsuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433969}}, Saburo Kobayashi, and Masaya Kumano (Editors), ''Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 1989. ISBN 4-490-10260-7 * ''Chiba Prefecture Place names (Japanese Historical Place names Series 12),'' Heibonsha, 1996. ISBN 978-4-582-49012-1 * {{ill|Reiji Harashima|ja|原島礼二|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reiji Harashima|1=Reiji Harashima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11409594}}, Ryoichi Kanaizuka (Editors), ''Eastern Japan and the Yamato Court,'' Yoshikawa Kobunkan, 1994. ISBN 4-642-02186-8, p. 141, "Human-shaped Haniwa of Ogawadai Kofun No. 5" == See Also == * [[Kofun period]] * [[:en:Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum|Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.haniwakan.com/tenji/1-4.html Shibayama Kofun Haniwa Museum Exhibit on Ogawadai Kofun No. 5] {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|小川台古墳群|version=89953380|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun clusters]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:小川台古墳群]] [[Category:Kofun of Chiba prefecture]] [[Category:Municipal History of Yokoshibahikari]] {{wikidata link|Q11460398}}
